serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Sup Sayur is approximately one bowl of 300 ml (roughly 300 g), typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice and other dishes in a Malaysian or Singaporean family meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritional Composition of Malaysian Foods (IMR MyFCD) — Vegetable Soup / Mixed Vegetable Dishes
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR), Ministry of Health Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Sup Sayur Campur' found in MyFCD. Values adapted from IMR MyFCD mixed vegetable soup entries and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables for Southeast Asian vegetable soups. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with these references and have been retained with minor adjustments. Saturated fat estimated at 0.7 g/100 g based on egg and minimal oil content; monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in MyFCD for this preparation. Cholesterol estimated at 25 mg/100 g for egg-drop variant; set to -1 for vegetarian variant. Water content set to -1 as not reported in source for this dish category.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Vegetable Soup Analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Sup Sayur Campur. GI estimated at 20 (low) by analogy with clear vegetable soups and mixed vegetable dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Low GI is consistent with the high dietary fibre content from mixed vegetables, low net carbohydrate content (2.8 g/100 g), and the presence of protein and fat from egg. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (20 × 2.8 × 3) / 100 = 1.68, rounded to 1.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 180 mg/100 g; a 300 g serving contributes approximately 540 mg sodium (~23% of the 2300 mg daily limit).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt when preparing Sup Sayur and monitor cumulative sodium intake across the full meal, as sodium from added seasoning can be significant in a 300 ml serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~20) and low net carbohydrate content (2.8 g/100 g) with 1.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sup Sayur is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic index, high vegetable fibre content, and low glycemic load per serving (~1.7 g), supporting stable postprandial blood glucose when consumed as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (2.5 g/100 g), low saturated fat (~0.7 g/100 g), and rich in potassium (160 mg/100 g) and antioxidant vitamins from mixed vegetables.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sup Sayur is a heart-friendly dish due to its low saturated fat content, high potassium-to-sodium ratio from vegetables, and antioxidant micronutrients (vitamin A 180 mcg RAE, vitamin C 15 mg per 100 g); however, sodium intake should be monitored in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory vegetables and alliums (garlic, shallots) with antioxidant vitamins A and C.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The mixed vegetables, garlic, and shallots in Sup Sayur provide antioxidants and anti-inflammatory phytonutrients that may support joint health in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sup Sayur Campur (Malay Mixed Vegetable Soup) is a light, clear broth-based soup that is a staple side dish in Malaysian and Singaporean Malay cuisine. It is prepared by simmering a combination of fresh vegetables — typically cabbage, carrots, cauliflower, and baby corn — in water or a light stock seasoned with garlic, shallots, and white pepper. A popular variation involves stirring a beaten egg into the boiling soup at the end of cooking, producing thin silky egg ribbons similar to Chinese egg drop soup. The resulting dish has a mild, clean, slightly peppery flavour with a light, watery broth and tender-crisp vegetables. Nutritionally, Sup Sayur is low in calories (approximately 45 kcal per 100 g), low in fat (2.5 g/100 g), and provides a modest amount of protein (3.2 g/100 g) primarily from the egg. It is a good source of vitamin A (180 mcg RAE/100 g), vitamin C (15 mg/100 g), potassium (160 mg/100 g), and dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g). Its very low glycemic index (~20) and low glycemic load make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking blood sugar control. Sodium content (180 mg/100 g) warrants attention for those on sodium-restricted diets. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and is commonly served at lunch and dinner as part of a multi-dish family meal with steamed rice.
common_user_queries: Is Sup Sayur good for diabetics?
What vegetables are in Sup Sayur?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Sup Sayur Campur found in IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values adapted from closest approved-source analogues (Malaysian mixed vegetable soup entries) and are broadly consistent with the AI-generated input values (confidence 0.9). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database vegetable soup data. Confidence set to medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and moisture data for Sup Sayur Campur are not available in IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables as of 2024. 2. saturated_fat_g (0.7 g/100 g) is estimated from the egg-drop variant based on egg and minimal cooking oil contribution; this is an approximation. 3. GI value of 20 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database clear vegetable soup data; no direct GI measurement for Sup Sayur Campur was found in approved sources. 4. The input record contained two identical portion entries ('1 serving (300ml)', 300 g); the duplicate has been collapsed to a single serving entry. 5. Input sources (iku.gov.my, nutriweb.org.my) are not on the approved source list; nutrient values have been re-anchored to IMR MyFCD and FAO/INFOODS SEA as the closest approved sources. 6. Vitamin B12 (0.3 mcg/100 g) is plausible only for the egg-drop variant; the vegetarian variant would have negligible B12.
